abstract: Using first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory, we investigate the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer MoS$_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. In stark contrast to graphene, which experiences a deterioration of $\mu$ by three orders of magnitude when patterned into nanoribbons, armchair MoS$_2$ nanoribbons maintain a mobility magnitude comparable to their 2D sheet counterpart, despite width-dependent oscillations. Remarkably, we observe a room-temperature transport polarity reversal between the two dimensions: while the 2D sheet exhibits higher hole mobility ($\mu_h \approx 200.52$ vs. $\mu_e \approx 72.16$ cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$), a 4 nm-wide nanoribbon favors electron transport ($\mu_e \approx 190.89$ vs. $\mu_h \approx 49.72$ cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$). Electronic structure analysis reveals that these robust mobility values and the unique polarity reversal are driven by the specific spatial distribution and orbital characteristics of edge states inherent to MoS$_2$ nanoribbons. Our findings suggest that lateral confinement combined with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for optimizing and tuning the transport properties of MoS$_2$-based nanodevices.
